Add `name` as a tie-breaker so the 5th row is chosen deterministically when salaries tie.
problems.explanation: ORDER BY + LIMIT is the top-N idiom; a deterministic tie-breaker matters because Priya and Raj both earn 120000.

problems.title: Second-highest distinct salary
problems.prompt: Return the second-highest **distinct** salary in the company as a single row, single column named `second_highest`.
problems.oracle: SELECT DISTINCT salary AS second_highest FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1
hints: Deduplicate salaries first (`DISTINCT`), then skip the top one with `OFFSET 1`.
The answer is a salary that exists in the data — 160000.
problems.explanation: Ranking must be over distinct salary values; otherwise duplicate top salaries would push the 'second' row to still be the max.
problems.wrongExample: SELECT MAX(salary) AS second_highest FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees) AND salary < 160000

problems.title: Out-earning your manager
problems.prompt: Find every employee who earns strictly more than their direct manager. Return columns `employee`, `emp_salary`, `manager`, and `mgr_salary`, ordered by `employee` ascending.
problems.oracle: SELECT e.name AS employee, e.salary AS emp_salary, m.name AS manager, m.salary AS mgr_salary FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON m.emp_id = e.manager_id WHERE e.salary > m.salary ORDER BY employee
hints: Self-join `employees` to itself: one alias for the employee, one for the manager.
Join condition is `manager.emp_id = employee.manager_id`, then filter `emp.salary > mgr.salary`.
problems.explanation: A self-join links each employee row to their manager's row via manager_id → emp_id; direction of the join condition matters.
problems.wrongExample: SELECT e.name AS employee, e.salary AS emp_salary, m.name AS manager, m.salary AS mgr_salary FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON m.manager_id = e.emp_id WHERE e.salary > m.salary ORDER BY employee

problems.title: Salary rank within department
problems.prompt: Rank employees by salary **within** their department using dense ranking (highest salary is rank 1; equal salaries share a rank with no gaps after). Return columns `department` (the department name), `name`, `salary`, and `salary_rank`. Order by `department` ascending, then `salary_rank` ascending, then `name` ascending.
problems.oracle: SELECT d.name AS department, e.name, e.salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY e.dept_id ORDER BY e.salary DESC) AS salary_rank FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id ORDER BY department, salary_rank, e.name
hints: `DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC)`.
DENSE_RANK gives tied rows the same rank and does NOT skip the next number (unlike RANK).
problems.explanation: DENSE_RANK partitions restart the counter per department; ties share a rank and the sequence continues without gaps.
problems.wrongExample: SELECT d.name AS department, e.name, e.salary, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY e.dept_id ORDER BY e.salary DESC) AS salary_rank FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id ORDER BY department, salary_rank, e.name

problems.title: Highest-paid employee per department
problems.prompt: Return exactly one employee — the highest paid — for each department. If salaries tie within a department, pick the one whose `name` comes first alphabetically. Return columns `department` (the department name), `name`, and `salary`, ordered by `department` ascending.
problems.oracle: SELECT department, name, salary FROM (SELECT d.name AS department, e.name, e.salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY e.dept_id ORDER BY e.salary DESC, e.name ASC) AS rn FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id) t WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY department
hints: Number rows within each department with `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC, name ASC)`, then keep `rn = 1`.
ROW_NUMBER (unlike RANK) breaks ties so exactly one row per department survives.
problems.explanation: ROW_NUMBER with a total ORDER BY inside the partition guarantees a single winner per department even when salaries tie.
problems.wrongExample: SELECT department, name, salary FROM (SELECT d.name AS department, e.name, e.salary, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY e.dept_id ORDER BY e.salary ASC, e.name ASC) AS rn FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id) t WHERE rn = 1 ORDER BY department

problems.title: Salary vs department average
problems.prompt: For each employee, compare their salary to the average salary of their department. Return columns `name`, `salary`, `dept_avg` (department average, rounded to 2 decimals), and `diff` (salary minus department average, rounded to 2 decimals). Order by `name` ascending.
problems.oracle: SELECT name, salary, ROUND(AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id), 2) AS dept_avg, ROUND(salary - AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id), 2) AS diff FROM employees ORDER BY name
hints: `AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id)` gives each row its department average without collapsing rows.
A window aggregate keeps every employee row, unlike `GROUP BY`.
problems.explanation: A windowed AVG attaches the group average to each individual row, so you can compute per-employee deviations without a self-join or GROUP BY.

problems.title: Running headcount by hire date
problems.prompt: As employees are hired over time, compute the cumulative company headcount after each hire. Return columns `hire_date`, `name`, and `running_headcount` (the number of employees hired on or before that person, ordered by hire_date). Hire dates are all distinct. Order by `hire_date` ascending.
problems.oracle: SELECT hire_date, name, COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY hire_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_headcount FROM employees ORDER BY hire_date
hints: `COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY hire_date)` produces a running count.
An ORDER BY inside the window turns the aggregate into a cumulative (running) total.
problems.explanation: A window function with ORDER BY accumulates over all rows up to the current one, giving a running total as of each hire date.

problems.title: Manager with the most direct reports
problems.prompt: Find the manager who has the most direct reports. Return columns `manager` (the manager's name) and `direct_reports` (count of employees reporting directly to them). Return only the single top manager; if there is a tie, the manager whose `name` comes first alphabetically wins.
problems.oracle: SELECT m.name AS manager, COUNT(*) AS direct_reports FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON m.emp_id = e.manager_id GROUP BY m.name ORDER BY direct_reports DESC, manager ASC LIMIT 1
hints: Self-join employees to their managers, then `GROUP BY manager` and `COUNT(*)`.
Order by the count descending, add a name tie-breaker, and `LIMIT 1`.
problems.explanation: Counting reports per manager requires grouping by the manager side of a self-join; the tie-breaker guarantees a single deterministic winner.

problems.title: Org hierarchy level
problems.prompt: Compute each employee's level in the org hierarchy. The CEO (no manager) is level 1; their direct reports are level 2; and so on. Return columns `name` and `level`, ordered by `level` ascending, then `name` ascending.
problems.oracle: WITH RECURSIVE org AS (SELECT emp_id, name, 1 AS level FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT e.emp_id, e.name, o.level + 1 FROM employees e JOIN org o ON e.manager_id = o.emp_id) SELECT name, level FROM org ORDER BY level, name
hints: Use `WITH RECURSIVE`: the anchor is the CEO (`manager_id IS NULL`) at level 1.
The recursive step joins each employee to the already-found row of their manager, adding 1 to the level.
problems.explanation: A recursive CTE walks the org tree top-down: the anchor seeds the root, and each iteration adds employees whose manager was found in the previous iteration.

problems.title: Share of department payroll
problems.prompt: For each employee, compute what fraction of their department's total payroll their salary represents. Return columns `department` (the department name), `name`, `salary`, and `payroll_share` — the ratio of the employee's salary to the department's total salary, rounded to 4 decimal places. Order by `department` ascending, then `payroll_share` descending, then `name` ascending.
problems.oracle: SELECT d.name AS department, e.name, e.salary, ROUND(e.salary / SUM(e.salary) OVER (PARTITION BY e.dept_id), 4) AS payroll_share FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id ORDER BY department, payroll_share DESC, e.name
hints: `SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id)` gives each row its department's total payroll.
Divide salary by that windowed total; cast to numeric if needed and `ROUND(..., 4)`.
problems.explanation: A windowed SUM supplies the department total on every row, so each employee's salary can be divided by it to get a within-department share.
problems.wrongExample: SELECT d.name AS department, e.name, e.salary, ROUND(e.salary / SUM(e.salary), 4) AS payroll_share FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON d.dept_id = e.dept_id GROUP BY d.name, e.name, e.salary ORDER BY department, payroll_share DESC, e.name
